Name: info_theoretic_compression
Title: Information-Theoretic Limits of Neural Network Compression
Short Hypothesis: The compression of neural networks can be optimized by leveraging information-theoretic principles, such as the information bottleneck method, to identify the fundamental limits of compression and develop novel compression algorithms that approach these limits.
Related Work: Recent works have explored the application of information-theoretic principles to neural network compression, including the use of mutual information and rate-distortion theory. However, these works have focused on specific aspects of compression, such as pruning or quantization, rather than providing a comprehensive understanding of the information-theoretic limits of compression. Our proposal builds on this existing work and aims to provide a more complete understanding of the fundamental limits of neural network compression.
Abstract: Neural networks are a key component of many modern machine learning systems, but their large size and computational requirements can make them difficult to deploy in resource-constrained environments. Compression techniques, such as pruning and quantization, can reduce the size and computational requirements of neural networks, but the optimal compression strategy is not well understood. In this proposal, we aim to develop a comprehensive understanding of the information-theoretic limits of neural network compression, leveraging techniques such as the information bottleneck method and rate-distortion theory. We will use this understanding to develop novel compression algorithms that approach the fundamental limits of compression, enabling the deployment of neural networks in a wide range of applications.
Experiments: We will conduct a series of experiments to validate our proposal, including: (1) an analysis of the information-theoretic limits of compression for a range of neural network architectures and datasets, (2) the development and evaluation of novel compression algorithms that leverage information-theoretic principles, and (3) a comparison of our compression algorithms with state-of-the-art techniques. We will use a range of evaluation metrics, including compression ratio, accuracy, and computational requirements.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The main risk factors and limitations of our proposal are: (1) the complexity of the information-theoretic analysis, which may require significant computational resources and expertise, (2) the potential for the compression algorithms to be sensitive to the specific neural network architecture and dataset, and (3) the need for careful evaluation and comparison with state-of-the-art techniques to ensure that our algorithms are effective and efficient.
